response:
blank The living room LED output function \( L(t) \) must compensate for the difference between the target 500 lumens and the available natural light \( N(t) \). blank Since total illumination equals natural light plus LED light, we set \( N(t) + L(t) = 500 \). blank Substituting the given natural light function yields \( L(t) = 500 - \left(300 + 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right)\right) \). blank Simplifying this expression results in \( L(t) = 200 - 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right) \) lumens. blank To find total energy, we integrate the power consumption over 24 hours, noting that LEDs consume 0.1 watts per lumen-hour. blank The total energy \( E \) is \( 0.1 \times \int_{0}^{24} L(t) \, dt \). blank Evaluating the integral of the sine term over a full period (24 hours) gives zero, leaving only the constant term. blank Thus, the integral of \( 200 \) over 24 hours is \( 200 \times 24 = 4800 \) lumen-hours. blank Multiplying by the power rate gives \( 0.1 \times 4800 = 480 \) watt-hours of total energy consumption.

blank The bedroom lighting constraint requires finding all integer triplets \( (r, g, b) \) where each value ranges from 0 to 255. blank The equation \( 0.3r + 0.6g + 0.1b = 500 \) defines the relationship between color intensities and total lumens. blank Multiplying the entire equation by 10 eliminates decimals to yield \( 3r + 6g + b = 5000 \). blank Solving for \( b \) gives \( b = 5000 - 3r - 6g \). blank Any valid combination must satisfy the condition that \( 0 \le b \le 255 \). blank This inequality restricts the possible values of \( r \) and \( g \) to a specific region within the 3D color space.
